activities_projects.title: Defining Deforestation
activities_projects.objective: Understand the basic concept of forest clearance and its scale
steps: Define the term 'deforestation' in your own words.
Identify one major reason mentioned in the text for why forests were cleared (e.g., Railways, Shipbuilding).
List two things we get from forests that disappear when trees are cut down.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the impact of large-scale tree felling on the environment.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear definition and identification of causes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Rise of Commercial Forestry
activities_projects.objective: Identify key figures and systems introduced by colonial rulers
steps: Locate the name of the first Inspector General of Forests in India (Dietrich Brandis).
Write down the name of the 'Scientific Forestry' system he introduced.
Draw a 'Forest Plantation' where only one type of tree is planted in straight rows.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the shift from natural forests to managed plantations.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the person and system (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Categorizing Forest Acts
activities_projects.objective: Understand how the British divided forests into different categories
steps: Identify the three categories of forests under the 1878 Act: Reserved, Protected, and Village forests.
Color-code them on a chart (e.g., Red for Reserved, Yellow for Protected, Green for Village).
Note which category was considered the 'best' and most restricted for villagers.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between levels of forest protection and restriction.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct categorization of the three forest types (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Rebellion in Bastar
activities_projects.objective: Identify the leaders and reasons for tribal resistance
steps: Find the name of the region in Chhattisgarh where the 1910 rebellion took place (Bastar).
Identify the leader associated with the movement (Gunda Dhur).
List one reason why the people were angry (e.g., forest reservations, forced labor).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that local communities fought to protect their forest rights.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate identification of the rebellion details (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Shift in Cultivation Practices
activities_projects.objective: Understand the traditional practice of 'Swidden' agriculture
steps: Define 'Shifting Cultivation' (also known as 'bewar' or 'penda').
Draw a simple diagram showing the cycle: cutting/burning, sowing, and leaving the land fallow.
List one reason why the British government wanted to ban this practice.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands traditional forest agriculture and colonial opposition to it.
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical explanation of the cultivation cycle (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Java: The Dutch Connection
activities_projects.objective: Learn about forest management in other parts of the world
steps: Identify the country where Java is located (Indonesia).
Name the colonial power that ruled Java (The Dutch).
Write down the name of the community known for being skilled forest cutters (The Kalangs).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Compares forest colonialism in India with that in Indonesia.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of geography and communities (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Forest Products Checklist
activities_projects.objective: Identify the various uses of forest resources in daily life
steps: Look around your house and find three items made from forest products (e.g., paper, furniture, spices).
List two 'minor forest products' that tribal people used to collect (e.g., honey, tendu leaves).
Explain how the Forest Acts made collecting these items 'illegal'.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates forest resources to everyday consumption and legal restrictions.
activities_projects.evaluation: Relevant list of products and explanation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Impact of Railways
activities_projects.objective: Understand how industrial growth led to forest destruction
steps: Identify how many sleepers were required for each mile of railway track.
Sketch a train on a track and label the 'wooden sleepers' beneath the rails.
Write a sentence on why the expansion of railways was bad for forests.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of data and clarity of the sketch (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Blandongdiensten System
activities_projects.objective: Identify specific labor systems used by the Dutch
steps: Read about the labor system introduced in Java.
Explain how villagers were exempted from land rent if they provided free labor.
List the types of work they had to do (e.g., cutting and transporting timber).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes forced labor as a tool of colonial forest management.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct explanation of the labor system (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: New Trends in Forestry
activities_projects.objective: Understand how modern forest management has changed
steps: Identify how conservation has replaced timber production in modern times.
Define 'Joint Forest Management' in a simple sentence.
List one way local communities are now involved in protecting forests.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the shift toward community-led forest conservation.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate description of modern trends (10 marks)
